bibliographic_data.title: Enforcing MAVLink Safety & Security Properties Via Refined Multiparty Session Types
bibliographic_data.abstract: A compromised system component can issue message sequences that are legal while also leading the overall system into unsafe states. Such stealthy attacks are challenging to characterize, because message interfaces in standard languages specify each individual message separately but do not specify safe sequences of messages. We present initial results from ongoing work applying refined multiparty session types as a mechanism for expressing and enforcing proper message usage to exclude unsafe sequences. We illustrate our approach by using refined multiparty session types to mitigate safety and security issues in the MAVLink protocol commonly used in UAVs.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper presents a methodology for preventing stealthy attacks against the MAVLink mission sub-protocol using refined multiparty session types (RMPSTs).
core_content.key_methodology: The authors use RMPSTs to formalize and enforce proper message usage in the MAVLink protocol, ensuring that only safe sequences of messages are allowed.
outcomes: Mitigation of safety and security issues in the MAVLink protocol used by UAVs.
Development of a methodology called Dynamically Assured Typed Universal Messaging (DATUM) for dynamically checking message conformance to protocols.
core_content.full_text_markdown:
The paper addresses stealthy attacks on UAV systems, where attackers can send seemingly innocuous messages that still cause damage. The authors propose using refined multiparty session types (RMPSTs) to enforce proper message usage and ensure safety in the MAVLink protocol. They formalize a significant portion of MAVLink and demonstrate its effectiveness through case studies with ArduPilot and PX4 autopilots.

The methodology, called Dynamically Assured Typed Universal Messaging (DATUM), checks that communication between controllers and controlled systems conforms to specified protocols. This approach can mitigate risks posed by stealthy attacks while patches are developed or validated.
